title: HeroUI v3 Lands as a Ground-Up Rewrite for React and React Native, Built on Tailwind CSS v4
gist: HeroUI (formerly NextUI), a Y Combinator-backed open-source React library, shipped a ground-up v3 rewrite unifying web and React Native on React Aria and Tailwind CSS v4, with heavy AI-assisted-development tooling (MCP server, Agent Skills, llms.txt), ~341K weekly npm downloads and ~27,700 GitHub stars, but a breaking migration and removed v2 features drawing user pushback.

insights.claim: HeroUI v3 is a ground-up rewrite framed as one design system spanning web and native, shipping 75+ web components (21 new) plus a from-scratch React Native library of 37 components.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: A single cross-platform design system reduces duplication for teams building web and mobile; signals consolidation pressure on separate web/native component libraries.
insights.quote: one design system for web and native. The March 2026 release ships more than 75 web components, 21 of them new, alongside a brand-new React Native library built from scratch with 37 components.
insights.locator: HeroUI, the React component library formerly known as NextUI

insights.claim: v3 is built on React Aria Components for accessibility and Tailwind CSS v4 for styling, exposing CSS variables, OKLCH colours and BEM modifiers, moving every animation to CSS with no JavaScript runtime and adopting a compound component API that drops the old provider wrapper and works with React 19 and Next.js.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: Zero-JS-runtime animations and provider-free compound APIs reflect the industry shift toward lighter, standards-based front-end primitives.
insights.quote: Every animation has moved to CSS with no JavaScript runtime, and the library adopts a compound component API that drops the old provider wrapper and works out of the box with React 19 and Next.js.
insights.locator: HeroUI v3 is built on React Aria Components

insights.claim: HeroUI v3 leans into AI-assisted development by shipping an MCP Server, Agent Skills and an llms.txt file so coding assistants can read component docs directly, plus a Figma Kit v3 with 1:1 component parity.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: Making component libraries machine-readable for coding agents is becoming table stakes; libraries without MCP/llms.txt risk lower adoption in AI-driven workflows.
insights.quote: shipping an MCP Server, Agent Skills and an llms.txt file so coding assistants can read component docs directly, plus a Figma Kit v3 with 1:1 component parity.
insights.locator: The rewrite also leans heavily into AI-assisted development

insights.claim: A dedicated migration MCP server accompanies the v2-to-v3 migration guide, applying AI tooling directly to the upgrade path.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: Purpose-built migration agents may become a standard mitigation for breaking major releases, easing upgrade friction.
insights.quote: The official migration guide offers both a full migration and an incremental migration path, supported by a dedicated migration MCP server
insights.locator: Teams moving from v2 should plan carefully

insights.claim: The core @heroui/react npm package records around 341,000 weekly downloads, the separate heroui-native package adds roughly 11,000 weekly, and the GitHub repository sits near 27,700 stars.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: Concrete adoption baseline; the ~30x gap between web and native downloads shows the React Native library is still nascent.
insights.quote: the core @heroui/react package now records around 341,000 weekly downloads, the separate heroui-native package adds roughly 11,000 a week, and the GitHub repository sits near 27,700 stars.

insights.claim: HeroUI is open-source under Apache 2.0, created by Junior García and backed by Y Combinator, with v3.2.0 (16 June 2026) as the current line and monthly minor releases now the stated cadence.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: YC backing plus a committed monthly release cadence signals sustained investment behind the library.
insights.quote: created by Junior García and backed by Y Combinator, distributed under the Apache 2.0 license
insights.locator: HeroUI is an open-source, production-ready React

insights.claim: Since 3.0, v3.0.3 dropped around 90 percent of React Aria Components' transitive dependencies for faster installs while adding a Typography component, and v3.1.0 added Chinese docs, expanded soft colour tokens, a themeable scrollbar and SSR fixes.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: Aggressive dependency pruning and rapid point releases indicate active maintenance and a focus on install performance.
insights.quote: v3.0.3 upgraded React Aria Components to 1.17.0 and dropped around 90 percent of its transitive dependencies for faster installs
insights.locator: Several point releases have landed since 3.0

insights.claim: v2 and v3 cannot coexist in the same project and some components such as Image have been removed entirely in favour of native elements, forcing teams to plan migrations carefully.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: The hard breaking change and removed components raise switching costs and may slow v3 adoption or push some users to alternatives.
insights.quote: v2 and v3 cannot coexist in the same project...some components such as Image have been removed entirely in favour of native elements.
insights.locator: Teams moving from v2 should plan carefully

insights.claim: Long-time v2 users report loved features are gone in v3, including input label animation choices, prebaked units on inputs and autocomplete, while GitHub developers cite missing pieces like Navbar and a lack of roadmap transparency.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: Feature regressions and roadmap opacity are the key adoption risks for v3; watch whether missing components return quickly.
insights.quote: I keep finding things missing which I loved in v2... The choice of animations for labels on inputs, the ability to have units prebaked into them, the autocomplete... all gone.
insights.locator: A long-time NextUI and v2 user writing on Reddit
